title: Beyond AI pilots, the organisations turning ambition into action
full_text:
IN PARTNERSHIP WITH Adobe Artificial intelligence has dominated boardroom conversations for the past two years. Yet despite the enthusiasm, many organisations remain in the same place. They have experimented with pilots, engineers, tested new tools and explored proofs of concept, but few have translated that momentum into meaningful business transformation.
The organisations pulling ahead are taking a different approach. Rather than treating AI as a tool, they are using it as an opportunity to rebuild the foundations that allow data, content and customer experiences to work together. Once this is done, AI becomes the accelerator, driving measurable returns by enhancing decision-making, operational efficiency and customer outcomes to improve the bottom line.
That shift was a defining theme at Adobe Summit Australia, where RACQ, one of Australia’s largest membership organisations, announced a five-year strategic partnership with Adobe and Deloitte Digital to help reshape how it serves more than 1.7 million members. More than a technology agreement, the partnership signals how established organisations are moving from AI experimentation to enterprise-wide transformation by connecting data, content, governance and customer experience around measurable outcomes.
For RACQ, that means using connected customer data with generative, predictive and agentic AI capabilities to deliver more personalised member experiences across insurance, banking, roadside assistance, travel, energy and mobility services. It also means establishing a governance and learning framework so emerging AI capabilities can be adopted responsibly, with a clear focus on member value.
As RACQ chief member and growth officer David Harrys said: “Our purpose is to drive a better future for all Queenslanders, and that shapes every decision we make.” That long-term perspective is exactly what successful AI transformation requires, because across every industry, customer expectations continue to rise. Whether renewing an insurance policy, opening a bank account, or choosing a university, people expect every interaction to feel relevant, timely and connected. They do not think in terms of departments, channels or legacy systems. They expect organisations to know them.
Delivering that experience has traditionally been difficult because many organisations still operate with customer information scattered across dozens of platforms. Marketing teams see one version of the customer. Customer service teams see another. Digital teams work from different datasets altogether.
Without a unified foundation, personalisation becomes difficult, reporting becomes unreliable and AI has little trustworthy information from which to generate meaningful insights.
RMIT University offered a complementary proof point, showing why that foundation matters before AI can scale. Darren Boyle, director of digital and experience, outlined the university’s work to better understand the prospective student journey from first interaction through to enrolment, after years of valuable customer data being spread across disconnected systems. Working with Adobe Professional Services, RMIT implemented a unified first party data foundation in just 15 weeks, connecting previously fragmented systems and establishing trusted reporting across the organisation.
For the first time, RMIT gained end-to-end visibility of prospective student journeys. AI-powered audience segmentation helped identify students with different likelihoods of enrolling, enabling more relevant communications across digital and offline channels. Website performance improved significantly after modernising code and tagging, while a targeted enrolment campaign achieved a 23 per cent conversion rate among students who had received offers but were yet to enrol.
Most importantly, RMIT created a trusted foundation that allows the university to confidently adopt the next generation of AI driven personalisation, automation and content creation. Reliable data has fundamentally changed how RMIT makes decisions, how quickly it responds, and how effectively they can engage prospective students throughout their journey.
Together, RACQ and RMIT show both sides of the same transformation arc. RACQ demonstrates how trusted foundations can scale AI-enabled experiences across complex member journeys, while RMIT shows the foundational work required before AI can create reliable, measurable impact.
The governance layer is critical to both examples. As AI adoption accelerates, organisations are discovering that success depends as much on operating models and organisational capability as it does on technology. Leaders increasingly need frameworks that prioritise use cases, establish trust in data, measure outcomes and ensure responsible deployment.
This is where many early AI initiatives have struggled. The technology itself has rarely been the limiting factor. More often, organisations lacked the connected data, governance or cross functional collaboration needed to move beyond experimentation.
The opportunity now is to rethink customer experience as an integrated system where data, creativity, AI and human expertise reinforce one another. For Australian organisations, the lesson is becoming increasingly clear.
Competitive advantage will not come simply from deploying more AI tools than competitors. It will come from creating the trusted digital foundations that allow those tools to generate better decisions, more relevant experiences and stronger business outcomes.
The organisations leading this next phase are already demonstrating what that future looks like. They are replacing fragmented customer journeys with connected experiences, turning data into actionable intelligence, and using AI to enhance how they engage customers.
The question for business leaders is no longer whether AI will reshape customer experience. It is whether their organisation has built the foundation needed to make that transformation real. Katrina Troughton, vice-president and managing director, Adobe Australia and New Zealand.
